subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Two distinct, fully labelled diagrams (price ceiling and tax) with correctly sloped curves, equilibrium points, and clear movements.
Comprehensive step-by-step explanations linking diagram shifts to article content and economic concepts, including elasticity effects and incentive changes.
weaknesses: Missing depiction of deadweightâloss triangles to show welfare impacts.
Could include a longârun supply curve to illustrate timeâlag effects visually.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission fulfils all topâband descriptors: it includes two relevant, accurate, correctly labelled diagrams with full explanations connected to article evidence. Minor stylistic omissions do not detract from its overall accuracy or clarity, meriting the full 3 marks.

strengths: Consistent and appropriate use of economic terminology (e.g., âprice ceiling,â âindirect tax,â âPED,â âPES,â âgovernment revenueâ).
Clear definitions and integration of key terms within explanations, enhancing clarity.
weaknesses: Occasional awkward phrasing (e.g., âgovernment trying to aid the significant increaseâ) that could be more precise.
Minor typos (âgreenhouse sightsâ) and informal language that slightly reduce polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Economic terminology is used accurately and consistently throughout, meeting the topâband requirements. The few language issues are minor and do not undermine overall clarity, justifying 2 marks.

strengths: Relevant economic theories (price controls, taxation, elasticity, incentives) are applied across the entire commentary.
Analysis identifies shortages, incentive effects, revenue use, and links policy tools to market outcomes with coherent reasoning.
weaknesses: Depth of welfare analysis (deadweight loss, distributional impacts) is limited.
Could specify tax type (ad valorem vs. perâunit) and quantify elasticities to strengthen precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While certain areas (welfare impacts, numerical elasticities) could be more developed, the breadth and coherence of theory application and analytical insight across multiple policy dimensions justify awarding the top mark for Application and Analysis.

strengths: The key concept of âgovernment interventionâ is explicitly identified and frontâloaded in the commentary.
There is a sustained, clear linkage between the concept and article evidence (housing unaffordability, rent control, land tax).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The commentary clearly and appropriately identifies the key concept and maintains a full, explicit connection to the article throughout, fully satisfying the highest strand criteria.

strengths: Balanced judgments on shortâ versus longâterm policy effects, weighing benefits and drawbacks.
Supporting reasoning accompanies each evaluative claim, demonstrating coherent logic.
weaknesses: Lacks stakeholderâlevel or quantitative evidence to deepen evaluation (e.g., distributional impacts, empirical case studies).
Could extend analysis to opportunity costs and externalities for a more comprehensive assessment.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student makes effective, balanced judgments and supports them with reasoning, but the evaluation remains at a general level and omits deeper quantitative or stakeholder analysis. This aligns with the secondâband descriptor, meriting 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Analysis moves beyond description, identifying shortages, incentive problems, revenue uses and time-lag issues.  However, the reasoning sometimes drifts (e.g., tax raising price yet âincreasing land availabilityâ) and omits welfare or stakeholder comparisons.  This places the work at âappropriate but could be more developedâ rather than the well-developed top band.

The student provides two distinct diagrams (price-ceiling and tax).  Both show correctly-sloped demand and supply curves, clearly labelled axes, equilibrium points, the price ceiling line (PC), shortage quantities (QSâQD), the original and post-tax supply curves (S and S+tax), and price/quantity movements (P1âP2; Q1âQ2).  Although minor stylistic omissions exist (dead-weight loss not included), the diagrams are fundamentally accurate and fully satisfy the rubric requirement for ârelevant, accurate and correctly-labelledâ diagrams.

Throughout the commentary the student explicitly links the concept of government intervention to the articleâs evidence (housing unaffordability, proposal of rent control, taxation of derelict land).  Each policy is related back to how intervention corrects the âsystemic failureâ, providing a clear, sustained connection that meets the top-band requirement for a âfull and clear explanationâ of the link.

Relevant theory (price controls, indirect taxation, elasticity, incentive effects, government revenue, market failure) is applied to every section of the article.  While the application is coherent, it lacks depth in areas such as welfare loss, long-run supply elasticity and alternative policy tools, so it is âapplied throughoutâ but not âthoroughlyâ enough for the top band.

Each evaluative claim is accompanied by some reasoningâe.g., price ceilings reduce quality by limiting profit; tax revenue can fund subsidies but may fall over time.  The logic is generally sound but not deeply developed (no distributional analysis, elasticity impacts, or empirical estimates).  Hence the reasoning is âeffective and balancedâ but not sufficiently detailed for the highest band.

The key concept âgovernment interventionâ is explicitly stated in the front-sheet and underpins the whole discussion of price ceilings and land taxes.  It is clearly appropriate for the articleâs focus on policy responses to housing market failure, satisfying the highest descriptor.

For each diagram the student offers a clear, step-by-step explanation: â¢ Price ceiling forces the price from PE to PC, creates a shortage (QDâQS) and reduces suppliersâ incentives; â¢ Tax shifts supply left, raises price to P2, lowers quantity to Q2 and generates government revenue.  The commentary also links the inelastic PED/PES of housing to the size of the shortage and discusses incentive effects and revenue use.  The explanations are accurate, go beyond a cursory statement and directly connect diagram movements to the article, fulfilling the âfull and accurate explanationâ descriptor.

Economic terms such as âprice ceilingâ, âindirect taxâ, âshortageâ, âPEDâ, âPESâ, âgovernment revenueâ, âsubsidyâ, and âmarket failureâ are used consistently and appropriately.  Occasional informal phrasing (âhousing is necessityâ) does not impede clarity, so the work meets the top-band standard for appropriate use throughout.

Judgments are made on both policies, weighing short-term benefits of price ceilings against their long-term drawbacks, and favouring land taxation as a more viable solution.  The discussion is balanced but remains fairly general and lacks stakeholder specificity or quantitative support, so it is âeffective and balancedâ yet not comprehensive enough to reach the highest standard.

annotations.comment: The definition of tax would be strengthened by specifying whether itâs an ad valorem or perâunit levy, clarifying the mechanism by which it shifts the supply curve.
The phrase âgovernment trying to aid the significant increaseâ is awkward; consider rephrasing to âto mitigate rising rentsâ for precision.
âGreenhouse sightsâ appears to be a typo. Verify the term âgreenhouse sitesâ to ensure accurate use of economic terminology.
Paraphrase key data (e.g., price-to-earnings multiple of 16.3) in the introduction to better integrate the articleâs evidence into your analysis.
Clear, concise definitions of taxation and price ceilings demonstrate appropriate use of economic terminology.
The price-ceiling diagram is accurately drawn and correctly labelled with PC, QD, and QS, effectively illustrating the shortage under inelastic housing supply.
Consider adding the deadweightâloss triangle and changes in consumer/producer surplus to deepen your diagrammatic analysis.
You state that PED and PES < 1 but do not quantify the elasticity; include at least a numerical estimate or formula for clarity.
The link between inelastic housing supply/demand and the resulting shortage is well articulated, showing solid application of theory.
Extend the evaluation by discussing potential welfare losses (e.g., reduced maintenance) with concrete examples.
The tax diagram is precisely labelled with SâS+tax, P1âP2 and Q1âQ2, clearly fulfilling diagram criteria.

The explanation that a leftward shift in supply increases land availability is conceptually flawed; a supply reduction lowers quantity, so availability depends on price incentives, not direct curve movement.
Use LaTeX to present the supply shift more clearly, e.g., write $$S \\rightarrow S + t$$ rather than inline HTML.
Replace informal wording like âtoo costlyâ with precise terms such as âincreased marginal cost,â improving terminology accuracy.
Assess potential externalities (e.g., landâuse spillovers) to evaluate whether the tax corrects the idleâland market failure.
The discussion of using tax revenue for housing subsidies shows strong application of policy analysis.
To improve evaluation, discuss concrete targeting mechanisms (e.g., means testing) for allocating subsidies to lowâincome renters.
Your point about revenue falling over time is valid; bolster it by citing empirical timeâseries data or specific case studies.
Include stakeholder analysis and opportunityâcost discussion when evaluating expansion of social rental housing.
Discuss the risk of fiscal crowding-out when the government increases housing expenditure at the expense of other public services.
Comparing the 8.7% social rental share to other jurisdictions is helpful; extend this by including international benchmarks for richer analysis.
The discussion of construction lags is apt; deepen this by analyzing the longârun supply elasticity and project timelines.
A long-run supplyâcurve diagram would enhance this argument by visually representing timeâlag effects.
The conclusion offers balanced judgments on shortâ versus longâterm interventions, demonstrating effective evaluation.
To strengthen your recommendation, outline a combined policy package with a timeline and transitional measures.

Full and accurate explanation is provided
No relevant economic terminology is used

Economic terminology relevant to the article is used appropriately throughout the commentary
No economic analysis is present
No relevant economic theory is applied
Relevant economic theory is applied with limited scope
Analysis is limited or superficial
Relevant economic theory is applied throughout the commentary
Analysis is appropriate but could be more developed
Relevant economic theory is thoroughly applied throughout the commentary
Analysis is effective and well-developed
No key concept is identified or concept has been used in another commentary
No attempt to link to the article
Basic attempt to link concept to the article
A key concept is identified
Partial explanation of link to the article
A key concept is clearly identified
Full and clear explanation of link to the article
A key concept is clearly and appropriately identified
No judgments are made
No supporting reasoning provided
Limited judgments are made
Limited reasoning supports the judgments
Effective and balanced judgments are made
Effective and balanced reasoning supports the judgments
Effective and balanced judgments are made
Effective and balanced reasoning supports the judgments
